The model evaluates the transaction features and returns a fraud score in milliseconds. DynamoDB stores transaction history for feature lookups (recent transaction patterns). The entire pipeline completes in under 100ms for real-time fraud scoring.
Option1: API Gateway to Lambda to SageMaker real-time endpoint with DynamoDB for feature lookups all within 100ms
Option2: Batch process transactions every hour for fraud detection
Option3: Use a rules-based system on EC2 without ML
Option4: Store transactions in S3 and analyze with Athena

QuestionText: A company's application uses a single-Region architecture and they need to add multi-Region disaster recovery with minimal changes to the existing application. Which approach provides DR with the LEAST application changes?
Explanation: AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS) continuously replicates EC2 instances to a DR Region. During a disaster, DRS launches instances from the replicated data in minutes. The application runs identically in the DR Region because DRS replicates the complete server state. Route 53 failover routing redirects traffic. This requires zero application changes—it replicates the entire infrastructure as-is.
Option1: Use AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery for continuous replication with Route 53 failover routing
Option2: Rewrite the application for multi-Region active-active
Option3: Take daily AMI snapshots and copy to another Region
Option4: Deploy a completely separate application stack in the DR Region

QuestionText: A company's application is experiencing memory leaks that cause EC2 instances to become unresponsive over time. Auto Scaling health checks don't detect the issue because the instances still pass EC2 status checks. Which solution detects and replaces unhealthy instances?
Explanation: Configuring the Auto Scaling group to use ELB health checks instead of EC2 status checks detects application-level issues. The ALB health check endpoint should verify application health (not just TCP connectivity)—checking memory usage, response time, and application state. When an instance becomes unresponsive due to memory leaks, it fails the ALB health check and Auto Scaling replaces it.
Option1: Configure Auto Scaling to use ELB health checks with an application-level health check endpoint that monitors memory usage
Option2: Increase the EC2 instance memory
Option3: Restart instances on a schedule
Option4: Disable Auto Scaling health checks

QuestionText: A company is migrating a legacy application that uses session affinity (sticky sessions) and stores state in local memory. They want to modernize for horizontal scalability during migration. Which TWO changes enable horizontal scaling? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Externalizing session state to ElastiCache Redis makes instances stateless—any instance can serve any request. Removing sticky session dependency from the ALB allows the load balancer to distribute traffic evenly across all instances. Together these changes enable true horizontal scaling where instances are interchangeable and can be added or removed without affecting user sessions.
Option1: Externalize session state to ElastiCache Redis
Option2: Remove sticky session configuration from the ALB
Option3: Add more memory to each instance
Option4: Use a Network Load Balancer instead of ALB

QuestionText: A company needs to migrate their on-premises Kubernetes workloads to AWS. They use custom Kubernetes operators and CRDs (Custom Resource Definitions). Which migration path preserves these customizations?
Explanation: Amazon EKS is fully Kubernetes-compatible and supports custom operators, CRDs, admission webhooks, and all standard Kubernetes APIs. Existing manifests, Helm charts, operators, and CRDs work on EKS without modification. The migration involves setting up an EKS cluster, deploying the custom operators, and migrating workloads. EKS manages the control plane while preserving all Kubernetes customizations.
Option1: Migrate to Amazon EKS which fully supports custom operators CRDs and all Kubernetes APIs
Option2: Migrate to ECS and rewrite operators as ECS task definitions
Option3: Migrate to Lambda and convert operators to Lambda functions
Option4: Install Kubernetes on EC2 without EKS

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their AWS environment meets HIPAA compliance requirements across all accounts. Which THREE controls are essential? (Select THREE)
Explanation: A BAA (Business Associate Agreement) with AWS is legally required before processing PHI on AWS. Encryption of PHI at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS) is a HIPAA technical safeguard. CloudTrail logging of all API access to PHI provides the audit trail required by HIPAA. All three are mandatory—missing any one means the environment is not HIPAA compliant.
Option1: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with AWS
Option2: Encrypt all PHI at rest and in transit
Option3: Enable CloudTrail logging for all accounts processing PHI
Option4: Store PHI in publicly accessible S3 buckets

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their AWS accounts have preventive guardrails that cannot be bypassed by account administrators. Which service provides this?
Explanation: AWS Control Tower guardrails (implemented as SCPs for preventive controls and Config rules for detective controls) cannot be bypassed by account administrators because SCPs are enforced at the Organizations level above individual account permissions. Even an account's root user cannot override an SCP. Control Tower manages these guardrails centrally with drift detection.
Option1: Use AWS Control Tower with preventive guardrails implemented as SCPs that override account-level permissions
Option2: Use IAM policies in each account
Option3: Use AWS Config rules only
Option4: Trust account administrators to follow policies

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their application teams can provision approved infrastructure patterns through a self-service portal with cost tracking per team. Which TWO services should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Service Catalog provides a self-service portal where teams select from pre-approved CloudFormation templates (products). Each product is tagged with the team's cost allocation tags automatically. AWS Budgets with per-team budgets and alerts tracks spending by team using the cost allocation tags. Together they provide governed self-service with per-team cost visibility and control.
Option1: AWS Service Catalog for self-service provisioning of approved infrastructure patterns
Option2: AWS Budgets with per-team cost tracking and alerts
Option3: Allow direct CloudFormation access for all teams
Option4: Use a shared spreadsheet for cost tracking

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their security team can centrally manage AWS WAF rules across all ALBs and CloudFront distributions in the organization. Which service provides this?
Explanation: AWS Firewall Manager centrally manages WAF rules across all accounts and resources in the organization. The security team defines WAF policies in the Firewall Manager administrator account, and the policies are automatically applied to all ALBs and CloudFront distributions across member accounts. New resources are automatically protected when created. This eliminates per-account WAF management.
Option1: Use AWS Firewall Manager to centrally manage WAF rules across all accounts and resources
Option2: Configure WAF rules individually on each ALB and CloudFront distribution
Option3: Use security groups instead of WAF
Option4: Deploy a third-party WAF in each account

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed search experience with natural language queries over structured and unstructured data. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Kendra provides intelligent enterprise search powered by ML. It understands natural language queries and returns precise answers (not just document links) from multiple data sources including S3, SharePoint, databases, and websites. Kendra uses NLP to understand query intent, provides ranked results with relevant passages, and supports access control based on user identity.
Option1: Use Amazon Kendra for intelligent enterprise search with natural language understanding
Option2: Build a custom search engine on Elasticsearch
Option3: Use Amazon CloudSearch for NLP queries
Option4: Use Athena for full-text search

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed Apache Airflow environment for orchestrating complex data pipelines. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow (MWAA) provides a fully managed Airflow environment. MWAA handles the Airflow infrastructure (web server, scheduler, workers, metadata database) while the developer focuses on writing DAGs. MWAA auto-scales workers based on workload, integrates with AWS services, and provides a secure, isolated environment for each customer.
Option1: Use Amazon MWAA for managed Apache Airflow with automatic scaling
Option2: Install Airflow on EC2 instances
Option3: Use Step Functions for all workflow orchestration
Option4: Use AWS Glue Workflows for complex DAGs

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed Redis cluster with automatic failover encryption and global replication for a session store used by a globally distributed application. Which service should they use?
Explanation: ElastiCache for Redis with Global Datastore provides cross-Region replication for Redis clusters. Each Region has a local Redis cluster for low-latency reads. Writes to the primary Region are replicated to all secondary Regions in under a second. Automatic failover promotes a secondary to primary if the primary Region fails. Encryption at rest and in transit is supported.
Option1: Use ElastiCache for Redis with Global Datastore for cross-Region replication with encryption and automatic failover
Option2: Deploy separate Redis clusters in each Region without replication
Option3: Use DynamoDB Global Tables for session storage
Option4: Use S3 for session storage with Cross-Region Replication

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed container registry with vulnerability scanning and image signing. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon ECR provides a managed Docker container registry with automatic vulnerability scanning (using Amazon Inspector), image signing (using Sigstore/Notation), lifecycle policies for image cleanup, and cross-Region replication. ECR integrates natively with ECS, EKS, and Lambda for container image deployment. Images are encrypted at rest and access is controlled through IAM policies.
Option1: Use Amazon ECR with vulnerability scanning image signing and lifecycle policies
Option2: Use Docker Hub for container image storage
Option3: Build a custom registry on EC2
Option4: Store container images in S3

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed service mesh for their microservices running on ECS and EKS. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: AWS App Mesh provides a managed service mesh that standardizes how microservices communicate. It uses Envoy proxies as sidecars to handle traffic routing, load balancing, circuit breaking, retries, observability (metrics, traces, logs), and mTLS encryption between services. App Mesh works across ECS, EKS, and EC2, providing consistent networking behavior regardless of compute platform.
Option1: Use AWS App Mesh for managed service mesh with Envoy proxies across ECS and EKS
Option2: Implement custom service-to-service communication logic
Option3: Use ALB for all inter-service communication
Option4: Deploy Istio on self-managed Kubernetes

QuestionText: A company's application uses a single-AZ RDS instance for a production database. They need to improve availability without significant application changes. Which TWO improvements should they make? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Enabling Multi-AZ creates a synchronous standby replica in a different AZ. Failover is automatic (60-120 seconds) and transparent to the application (same DNS endpoint). Enabling automated backups with point-in-time recovery provides protection against data corruption or accidental deletion. Both improve availability and durability with no application changes required.
Option1: Enable RDS Multi-AZ for automatic failover
Option2: Enable automated backups with point-in-time recovery
Option3: Migrate to DynamoDB for better availability
Option4: Deploy a second RDS instance and manage replication manually

QuestionText: A company's application is experiencing slow page loads. Analysis shows the application makes 50 API calls to render a single page. Which architectural improvement reduces page load time?
Explanation: Implementing a Backend for Frontend (BFF) pattern creates an aggregation layer that combines multiple backend API calls into a single response. The BFF makes the 50 API calls in parallel (or caches results), aggregates the data, and returns a single optimized response to the frontend. This reduces the number of round trips from 50 to 1, dramatically improving page load time.
Option1: Implement a BFF aggregation layer that combines multiple API calls into a single optimized response
Option2: Increase the bandwidth of the internet connection
Option3: Add more backend servers
Option4: Cache the entire page in the browser indefinitely

QuestionText: A company's Lambda functions are hitting the account-level concurrency limit during peak hours causing throttling for all functions. Which TWO solutions address this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Reserved concurrency on critical functions guarantees a portion of the account's concurrency for those functions, preventing less important functions from consuming all available concurrency. Requesting a concurrency limit increase from AWS provides more total capacity for the account. Together these ensure critical functions always have capacity while increasing overall headroom.
Option1: Configure reserved concurrency on critical Lambda functions
Option2: Request an account-level concurrency limit increase from AWS
Option3: Reduce the number of Lambda functions
Option4: Move all functions to EC2

QuestionText: A company's application stores large binary files (images videos) in an RDS database as BLOBs causing database performance issues. Which solution improves performance?
Explanation: Moving binary files to S3 and storing only the S3 object key (URL) in the database dramatically reduces database size and I/O. S3 is purpose-built for object storage with virtually unlimited capacity. The database handles only metadata (small rows), improving query performance. CloudFront can serve the S3 objects with low latency. This is a fundamental architectural best practice.
Option1: Move binary files to S3 and store S3 object references in the database with CloudFront for delivery
Option2: Increase the RDS storage size
Option3: Add read replicas for BLOB queries
Option4: Compress the BLOBs before storing in the database

QuestionText: A company wants to implement a solution that automatically detects and remediates configuration drift in their CloudFormation-managed infrastructure. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: CloudFormation drift detection identifies resources whose actual configuration differs from the template definition. AWS Config rules can continuously monitor for drift. When drift is detected, a Lambda function (triggered by Config or EventBridge) can either alert the team or automatically redeploy the CloudFormation stack to remediate the drift. This maintains infrastructure consistency.
Option1: Use CloudFormation drift detection with automated remediation via Lambda to redeploy drifted stacks
Option2: Manually compare resources to templates monthly
Option3: Ignore drift since it doesn't affect functionality
Option4: Delete and recreate all stacks weekly

QuestionText: A company is migrating a legacy application that uses a commercial ESB (Enterprise Service Bus) for integration. They want to replace it with AWS-native services. Which TWO services replace ESB functionality? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Amazon EventBridge replaces the ESB's event routing and transformation capabilities—it routes events between services based on content-based rules with built-in transformation. Step Functions replaces the ESB's workflow orchestration—coordinating multi-step processes with error handling, retries, and human approval steps. Together they provide the core ESB capabilities without commercial licensing costs.
Option1: Amazon EventBridge for event routing and transformation
Option2: AWS Step Functions for workflow orchestration
Option3: Amazon MQ for ESB replacement
Option4: Amazon SQS for all ESB functionality

QuestionText: A company is migrating a legacy application that uses a shared file system (NFS) accessed by multiple application servers. The file system contains 20 TB of data. Which migration approach should they use?
Explanation: Amazon EFS provides a managed NFS file system compatible with the existing NFS protocol. AWS DataSync automates the data transfer from on-premises NFS to EFS with integrity verification, handling the 20 TB migration efficiently. DataSync supports incremental transfers for ongoing synchronization during the migration period. Applications mount EFS using the same NFS protocol with only an endpoint change.
Option1: Migrate to Amazon EFS using AWS DataSync for automated data transfer with incremental sync
Option2: Copy files to S3 and modify applications to use S3 API
Option3: Install NFS server on EC2 and manually copy files
Option4: Use AWS Snowball for file system migration

QuestionText: A company is modernizing their application and wants to implement a CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) pattern. Which AWS architecture supports this?
Explanation: CQRS separates read and write models. DynamoDB handles writes (commands) with its fast, scalable write performance. DynamoDB Streams captures all changes and triggers Lambda functions that update read-optimized views in ElastiCache (for fast lookups) or OpenSearch (for complex queries). The read model is eventually consistent with the write model but optimized for each query pattern.
Option1: DynamoDB for writes with Streams triggering Lambda to update read-optimized views in ElastiCache and OpenSearch
Option2: A single RDS database for both reads and writes
Option3: S3 for all data storage
Option4: Separate RDS instances with manual synchronization

QuestionText: A company needs to migrate their on-premises Active Directory-integrated applications to AWS. The applications require AD authentication. Which approach maintains AD compatibility with the LEAST operational overhead?
Explanation: AWS Managed Microsoft AD provides a fully managed Active Directory in the cloud. It supports AD-aware applications, Group Policy, trusts with on-premises AD, and LDAP. AWS manages the domain controllers, patching, replication, and backups. Applications authenticate against Managed AD using the same protocols (Kerberos, LDAP) as on-premises. A trust relationship with on-premises AD enables seamless authentication.
Option1: Use AWS Managed Microsoft AD with a trust relationship to on-premises AD
Option2: Install Active Directory on EC2 instances
Option3: Migrate all applications to Cognito authentication
Option4: Use AD Connector without any managed AD

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed time-series database for IoT sensor data with built-in analytics functions. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Timestream is a purpose-built time-series database that provides fast ingestion, storage tiering (in-memory for recent data, magnetic for historical), and built-in analytics functions (interpolation, smoothing, approximation). Timestream automatically manages data lifecycle, moving data between storage tiers based on age. It scales to trillions of events per day with SQL-compatible queries.
Option1: Use Amazon Timestream for managed time-series storage with built-in analytics and automatic data tiering
Option2: Use DynamoDB with TTL for time-series data
Option3: Use RDS with timestamp-based partitioning
Option4: Use S3 with Athena for time-series queries

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed blockchain network for supply chain tracking. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Managed Blockchain provides fully managed blockchain networks using Hyperledger Fabric or Ethereum. For supply chain tracking, Hyperledger Fabric provides a permissioned blockchain where known participants (suppliers, manufacturers, distributors) record transactions. Managed Blockchain handles node provisioning, certificate management, and network scaling. Smart contracts (chaincode) enforce business rules.
Option1: Use Amazon Managed Blockchain with Hyperledger Fabric for permissioned supply chain tracking
Option2: Build a custom blockchain on EC2
Option3: Use DynamoDB for supply chain tracking
Option4: Use S3 for immutable record storage

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their AWS environment supports multiple compliance frameworks (SOC 2 PCI DSS HIPAA) simultaneously across different account groups. Which approach provides this?
Explanation: Different OUs can have different SCPs and Config conformance packs applied. PCI DSS accounts go in a PCI OU with PCI-specific SCPs and conformance packs. HIPAA accounts go in a HIPAA OU with HIPAA-specific controls. SOC 2 controls apply organization-wide. Security Hub evaluates compliance against multiple frameworks simultaneously. This layered approach supports multiple compliance frameworks.
Option1: Use OUs with framework-specific SCPs and Config conformance packs plus Security Hub for multi-framework compliance evaluation
Option2: Apply all compliance controls to all accounts regardless of requirements
Option3: Use a single OU for all compliance frameworks
Option4: Implement compliance controls only in production accounts

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their AWS accounts can access shared services (Active Directory DNS monitoring) hosted in a central account without internet exposure. Which networking architecture provides this?
Explanation: A shared services VPC in the central account hosts AD, DNS, and monitoring. Transit Gateway connects all workload VPCs to the shared services VPC. VPC endpoints in the shared services VPC provide private access to AWS services. Route 53 Resolver rules (shared via RAM) forward DNS queries appropriately. All traffic stays within the AWS private network—no internet exposure.
Option1: Central shared services VPC connected to all workload VPCs via Transit Gateway with private DNS and VPC endpoints
Option2: Expose shared services through public endpoints
Option3: Deploy shared services in every workload account
Option4: Use VPN connections between each account and the shared services VPC

QuestionText: A company's application uses a polling pattern where Lambda functions check an SQS queue every minute via CloudWatch Events. This is inefficient and adds latency. Which improvement eliminates polling?
Explanation: SQS event source mappings for Lambda eliminate polling entirely. Lambda automatically polls the SQS queue and invokes the function when messages are available. This is more efficient (no wasted invocations when the queue is empty), lower latency (messages are processed immediately), and simpler (no CloudWatch Events rule needed). Lambda handles the polling, batching, and error handling automatically.
Option1: Replace the CloudWatch Events polling pattern with an SQS event source mapping on the Lambda function
Option2: Increase the polling frequency to every second
Option3: Switch to SNS for push-based delivery
Option4: Use EC2 instances for queue processing

QuestionText: A company is migrating a legacy application that uses stored procedures heavily in an Oracle database. Many stored procedures contain complex business logic. Which approach handles the stored procedure migration?
Explanation: AWS SCT converts Oracle PL/SQL stored procedures to PostgreSQL PL/pgSQL. SCT identifies procedures that can be automatically converted and flags those requiring manual intervention. For complex procedures that are difficult to convert, the business logic can be extracted into Lambda functions or application code, modernizing the architecture while migrating. SCT provides an assessment report showing conversion complexity.
Option1: Use AWS SCT to convert stored procedures with manual intervention for complex cases and optionally extract logic to Lambda
Option2: Rewrite all stored procedures from scratch
Option3: Keep the Oracle database for stored procedures only
Option4: Abandon all stored procedure logic

QuestionText: A company needs to migrate their on-premises Redis cluster to AWS. They require zero-downtime migration. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: ElastiCache for Redis supports online migration from self-managed Redis. The migration tool replicates data from the source Redis cluster to ElastiCache in real-time. Once replication catches up, the application is switched to the ElastiCache endpoint. The cutover window is seconds—just enough time to redirect the application. This provides near-zero downtime for the Redis migration.
Option1: Use ElastiCache for Redis online migration to replicate data in real-time with near-zero downtime cutover
Option2: Export Redis data to RDB file and import into ElastiCache
Option3: Manually recreate all Redis data in ElastiCache
Option4: Use DMS for Redis migration

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their AWS accounts cannot create IAM users with console access—all human access must go through IAM Identity Center. Which approach enforces this?
Explanation: An SCP that denies iam:CreateUser, iam:CreateLoginProfile, and iam:CreateAccessKey (with an exception for the CI/CD pipeline role) prevents any account from creating IAM users with console or programmatic access. All human access is forced through IAM Identity Center, which provides centralized authentication, MFA enforcement, and temporary credentials. This eliminates long-term credentials across the organization.
Option1: Apply an SCP denying IAM user creation actions with an exception for CI/CD roles forcing all human access through Identity Center
Option2: Ask account administrators to not create IAM users
Option3: Use AWS Config to detect IAM users after creation
Option4: Disable the IAM service in all accounts

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their VPC IP address ranges do not overlap across 200 accounts to enable Transit Gateway connectivity. Which approach manages IP allocation centrally?
Explanation: Amazon VPC IP Address Manager (IPAM) provides centralized IP address planning and allocation across the organization. IPAM pools define IP ranges that are allocated to accounts and Regions. When a new VPC is created, it draws from the IPAM pool, ensuring no overlaps. IPAM integrates with Organizations for automatic scope management and provides compliance monitoring for IP usage.
Option1: Use Amazon VPC IPAM for centralized IP address allocation across the organization
Option2: Maintain a spreadsheet of IP allocations
Option3: Use the same CIDR range for all VPCs
Option4: Let each team choose their own IP ranges

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their data lake in a central account can be accessed by analytics teams in multiple accounts with fine-grained access control. Which approach provides this?
Explanation: AWS Lake Formation provides centralized data governance for data lakes. The data lake is stored in S3 in a central account. Lake Formation grants cross-account access with column-level and row-level permissions. Analytics teams in other accounts create resource links to shared tables and query them through Athena or Redshift Spectrum. All access is governed by Lake Formation permissions.
Option1: Use AWS Lake Formation for cross-account data sharing with fine-grained column and row-level access control
Option2: Copy data to each analytics account
Option3: Make the S3 bucket public for cross-account access
Option4: Use S3 bucket policies for column-level access control

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed API for mobile applications with offline support authentication and real-time data synchronization. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: AWS AppSync provides a managed GraphQL API with offline support (client-side caching with automatic conflict resolution when reconnected), Cognito integration for authentication, and real-time subscriptions for live data updates. DynamoDB as the data source provides single-digit millisecond performance. This architecture is purpose-built for mobile applications requiring offline-first capabilities.
Option1: AWS AppSync with Cognito authentication DynamoDB backend and real-time subscriptions for offline-capable mobile apps
Option2: API Gateway REST API with Lambda and RDS
Option3: EC2 instances running a custom sync server
Option4: S3 with CloudFront for API hosting

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides blue/green deployments for their entire infrastructure stack including databases. Which approach provides the safest infrastructure-level blue/green deployment?
Explanation: CloudFormation with a blue/green strategy creates a complete new stack (green) alongside the existing stack (blue). Route 53 weighted routing gradually shifts traffic from blue to green. Aurora cloning creates a fast copy of the database for the green environment. If issues are detected, traffic is shifted back to blue instantly. After validation, the blue stack is decommissioned.
Option1: CloudFormation parallel stacks with Route 53 weighted routing and Aurora cloning for database blue/green deployment
Option2: Update the existing stack in place
Option3: Deploy green resources in the same stack
Option4: Use a single database shared between blue and green

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed message broker supporting both MQTT and AMQP protocols for IoT and enterprise messaging. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: AWS IoT Core handles MQTT for IoT device communication with millions of concurrent connections. Amazon MQ (RabbitMQ) handles AMQP for enterprise messaging with guaranteed delivery and complex routing. EventBridge bridges the two systems—IoT Core rules forward relevant IoT messages to EventBridge, which routes them to Amazon MQ topics for enterprise consumers. This hybrid approach supports both protocols natively.
Option1: IoT Core for MQTT with Amazon MQ for AMQP bridged through EventBridge for cross-protocol messaging
Option2: Use a single Amazon MQ instance for both protocols
Option3: Build a custom message broker on EC2
Option4: Use SQS for all messaging regardless of protocol

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed Kubernetes platform with automatic node scaling GPU support and integration with AWS services. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: Amazon EKS with Karpenter (or Cluster Autoscaler) provides automatic node scaling based on pod resource requests. EKS supports GPU instance types (P4, G5) for ML workloads. Native AWS integrations include ALB Ingress Controller, EBS CSI driver, EFS CSI driver, IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA), and CloudWatch Container Insights. This provides a fully integrated managed Kubernetes platform.
Option1: Amazon EKS with Karpenter for auto-scaling GPU instance support and native AWS service integrations
Option2: Self-managed Kubernetes on EC2
Option3: ECS for Kubernetes workloads
Option4: Lambda for container orchestration

QuestionText: A company's application uses a monolithic deployment where all features are deployed together. A bug in one feature causes the entire application to fail. Which improvement reduces blast radius?
Explanation: Feature flags (using AWS AppConfig or a feature flag service) allow individual features to be enabled or disabled without redeployment. If a feature causes issues, it can be instantly disabled while the rest of the application continues functioning. This reduces blast radius from 'entire application' to 'single feature' and enables gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and instant rollback.
Option1: Implement feature flags using AWS AppConfig to enable instant feature-level rollback without redeployment
Option2: Deploy all features together but test more thoroughly
Option3: Maintain separate branches for each feature
Option4: Deploy less frequently to reduce risk

QuestionText: A company's application uses synchronous database writes that block the API response. Write operations take 500ms causing slow API responses. Which architectural improvement reduces API response time?
Explanation: Implementing an asynchronous write pattern using SQS decouples the API response from the database write. The API validates the request, sends a message to SQS, and returns a 202 Accepted response immediately (under 50ms). A Lambda consumer processes the SQS message and performs the database write asynchronously. This reduces API response time from 500ms to under 50ms.
Option1: Implement asynchronous writes using SQS to decouple the API response from the database write
Option2: Increase the database instance size
Option3: Add database connection pooling
Option4: Use a faster programming language

QuestionText: A company's EKS cluster is running out of IP addresses in the VPC subnet. Pods are failing to schedule due to insufficient IPs. Which TWO solutions address this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: EKS with the VPC CNI plugin's prefix delegation mode assigns /28 prefixes (16 IPs) to each ENI slot instead of individual IPs, dramatically increasing the number of available pod IPs per node. Alternatively, using a larger subnet CIDR (or adding secondary CIDRs to the VPC) provides more IP addresses. Both address IP exhaustion from different angles.
Option1: Enable VPC CNI prefix delegation to increase available pod IPs per node
Option2: Add secondary CIDR ranges to the VPC and create larger subnets
Option3: Reduce the number of pods per node
Option4: Switch to a different CNI plugin

QuestionText: A company wants to implement a solution that provides automated cost optimization recommendations specific to their workload patterns. Which AWS service provides this?
Explanation: AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes CloudWatch metrics for EC2, Auto Scaling groups, Lambda, EBS, and ECS on Fargate. It uses ML to identify optimal resource configurations based on actual utilization patterns. Recommendations include right-sizing (smaller instances), instance family changes (Graviton), and EBS volume type changes. Each recommendation includes projected savings and performance impact.
Option1: Use AWS Compute Optimizer for ML-based resource optimization recommendations
Option2: Manually analyze CloudWatch metrics for each resource
Option3: Use AWS Trusted Advisor for all optimization needs
Option4: Reduce all resources to the smallest size

QuestionText: A company's application experiences periodic spikes in DynamoDB write traffic that cause throttling. The spikes are predictable (every day at 9 AM). Which approach prevents throttling while optimizing cost?
Explanation: DynamoDB scheduled auto-scaling (using Application Auto Scaling with scheduled actions) pre-scales write capacity before the predicted spike. At 8:55 AM, capacity scales up to handle the 9 AM spike. After the spike subsides, capacity scales back down. This prevents throttling during the spike while avoiding the cost of maintaining high capacity 24/7. Scheduled scaling is more responsive than reactive auto-scaling for predictable patterns.
Option1: Configure DynamoDB scheduled auto-scaling to pre-scale capacity before the predictable daily spike
Option2: Set provisioned capacity to peak levels 24/7
Option3: Switch to on-demand capacity mode
Option4: Accept throttling during the spike

QuestionText: A company is migrating a legacy application that uses CRON jobs on a Linux server for scheduled tasks. Which AWS service replaces CRON with a managed serverless alternative?
Explanation: Amazon EventBridge Scheduler provides managed scheduling with cron and rate expressions. Each schedule triggers a target (Lambda, Step Functions, SQS, etc.) at the specified time. EventBridge Scheduler is serverless—no servers to manage, automatic scaling, and built-in retry logic. This replaces self-managed CRON with a fully managed, highly available scheduling service.
Option1: Use Amazon EventBridge Scheduler with Lambda for managed serverless scheduled tasks
Option2: Install CRON on an EC2 instance
Option3: Use CloudWatch Events only for scheduling
Option4: Run scheduled tasks manually

QuestionText: A company is migrating a legacy application that uses FTP for file transfers with external partners. They want to modernize while maintaining FTP compatibility for partners who cannot change. Which AWS service provides this?
Explanation: AWS Transfer Family provides fully managed FTP, FTPS, and SFTP servers that store files directly in S3 or EFS. External partners continue using their existing FTP clients—only the server endpoint changes. Transfer Family handles authentication (AD, custom Lambda), authorization, and scaling. Files land directly in S3 for processing by Lambda, Glue, or other AWS services.
Option1: Use AWS Transfer Family for managed FTP/SFTP with S3 backend storage
Option2: Run an FTP server on EC2
Option3: Ask all partners to switch to S3 API
Option4: Use AWS DataSync for partner file transfers

QuestionText: A company is modernizing their application and wants to implement the saga pattern for distributed transactions across microservices. Which AWS service orchestrates sagas?
Explanation: Step Functions orchestrates the saga pattern by coordinating a sequence of local transactions across microservices. Each step invokes a microservice's transaction. If any step fails, Step Functions executes compensating transactions (rollbacks) for all previously completed steps in reverse order. The Catch and Retry blocks handle failures, and the visual workflow makes the saga easy to understand and debug.
Option1: Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate the saga pattern with compensating transactions for rollback
Option2: Use distributed two-phase commit across all services
Option3: Use a single database transaction spanning all services
Option4: Ignore transaction consistency across services

QuestionText: A company needs to migrate their on-premises Elasticsearch cluster to AWS. They want a managed service with minimal migration effort. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: Amazon OpenSearch Service (successor to Amazon Elasticsearch Service) is API-compatible with Elasticsearch. The migration involves creating an OpenSearch domain, taking a snapshot of the on-premises Elasticsearch cluster, uploading it to S3, and restoring it to OpenSearch. Existing Elasticsearch clients, queries, and dashboards work with OpenSearch with minimal changes. OpenSearch manages infrastructure, scaling, and patching.
Option1: Migrate to Amazon OpenSearch Service using snapshot and restore from the on-premises Elasticsearch cluster
Option2: Install Elasticsearch on EC2 instances
Option3: Rewrite all queries for a different search engine
Option4: Use Amazon CloudSearch instead

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed workflow for ETL data quality checks and ML model training in a single pipeline. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: SageMaker Pipelines provides a managed ML workflow that integrates data processing (Glue or SageMaker Processing), data quality checks (SageMaker Data Quality), model training (SageMaker Training), model evaluation, and model deployment into a single automated pipeline. Each step is tracked with lineage, and the pipeline can be triggered on schedule or by events.
Option1: Use SageMaker Pipelines for end-to-end ML workflows integrating data processing quality checks training and deployment
Option2: Build a custom pipeline with Lambda and Step Functions
Option3: Run all steps manually in Jupyter notebooks
Option4: Use separate tools for each pipeline step

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed service for running Apache Spark workloads without managing clusters. Which TWO AWS services provide serverless Spark? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Glue provides serverless Spark for ETL workloads—no cluster management, automatic scaling, and pay-per-use pricing. Amazon EMR Serverless provides serverless Spark (and Hive) for broader analytics workloads with more configuration control than Glue. Both eliminate cluster management while providing Spark compatibility. Glue is simpler for ETL; EMR Serverless offers more flexibility for complex Spark applications.
Option1: AWS Glue for serverless Spark ETL
Option2: Amazon EMR Serverless for serverless Spark analytics
Option3: Amazon Athena for Spark workloads
Option4: Lambda for Spark processing

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their AWS environment supports both IPv4 and IPv6 for a gradual transition to IPv6. Which VPC configuration supports this?
Explanation: Dual-stack VPCs support both IPv4 and IPv6 simultaneously. Each subnet can be configured with both an IPv4 CIDR and an IPv6 CIDR. Instances receive both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses. Security groups and NACLs support both protocols. This enables a gradual transition where applications can be updated to use IPv6 while maintaining IPv4 compatibility for legacy systems.
Option1: Configure dual-stack VPCs with both IPv4 and IPv6 CIDR blocks on subnets
Option2: Use only IPv6 and drop IPv4 support immediately
Option3: Use NAT64 for all traffic
Option4: Maintain separate VPCs for IPv4 and IPv6

QuestionText: A company's application uses a single large Lambda function that handles multiple API endpoints. The function is 250 MB and has 10-second cold starts. Which TWO improvements reduce cold start latency? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Splitting the monolithic function into smaller single-purpose functions reduces deployment package size, which directly reduces cold start time (less code to load and initialize). Using Lambda SnapStart (for Java) or Provisioned Concurrency pre-initializes execution environments, eliminating cold starts entirely for pre-warmed instances. Both address cold starts from different angles—smaller packages and pre-initialization.
Option1: Split the monolithic function into smaller single-purpose functions to reduce package size
Option2: Use Provisioned Concurrency or SnapStart to pre-initialize execution environments
Option3: Increase the Lambda memory to 10 GB
Option4: Move to EC2 for all API handling

QuestionText: A company is migrating a legacy application that uses Oracle Golden Gate for database replication. They want to replace it with an AWS-native solution. Which service provides similar CDC replication capabilities?
Explanation: AWS DMS with CDC (Change Data Capture) provides continuous replication similar to Oracle Golden Gate. DMS reads the source database's transaction log and replicates changes to the target in near real-time. DMS supports Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, and other databases as sources and targets. It handles schema conversion (with SCT), initial load, and ongoing replication.
Option1: Use AWS DMS with CDC for continuous database replication replacing Oracle Golden Gate
Option2: Continue using Golden Gate on EC2
Option3: Use S3 replication for database changes
Option4: Implement custom replication with Lambda

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their AWS environment can detect and alert on cost anomalies such as unexpected spending spikes across all accounts. Which service provides this?
Explanation: AWS Cost Anomaly Detection uses ML to identify unusual spending patterns across accounts, services, and cost allocation tags. It automatically establishes spending baselines and alerts when actual spending deviates significantly. Alerts are sent via SNS with details about the anomaly (which account, service, and the magnitude of the deviation). This provides proactive cost monitoring without manual threshold configuration.
Option1: Use AWS Cost Anomaly Detection for ML-based identification of unusual spending patterns across the organization
Option2: Set static budget thresholds for every service in every account
Option3: Review billing reports manually each month
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their AWS accounts can only use specific EC2 instance types approved by the architecture team. Which TWO approaches enforce this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: An SCP with a Deny statement on ec2:RunInstances where the ec2:InstanceType condition is NOT in the approved list prevents launching unapproved instance types across all accounts. Service Catalog products with pre-configured instance types ensure developers can only launch approved configurations through the self-service portal. Both provide preventive enforcement at different levels.
Option1: Apply an SCP denying ec2:RunInstances for unapproved instance types
Option2: Use Service Catalog products with pre-approved instance type configurations
Option3: Use AWS Config to detect unapproved instances after launch
Option4: Send email reminders about approved instance types

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed service for running Windows containers. Which TWO AWS services support Windows containers? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Amazon ECS supports Windows containers on EC2 launch type with Windows Server AMIs. Amazon EKS supports Windows worker nodes alongside Linux nodes in the same cluster, enabling mixed workloads. Both services provide managed container orchestration for Windows containers with integration to other AWS services like ALB, CloudWatch, and ECR.
Option1: Amazon ECS with EC2 launch type using Windows Server instances
Option2: Amazon EKS with Windows worker nodes
Option3: AWS Fargate for Windows containers
Option4: AWS Lambda for Windows containers

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed graph database for a social networking application with complex relationship queries. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Neptune is a fully managed graph database optimized for storing and querying highly connected data. It supports Property Graph (Gremlin) and RDF (SPARQL) query languages. For social networking, Neptune efficiently handles queries like 'friends of friends', 'shortest path between users', and 'recommendation based on connections'—queries that would be extremely complex and slow in relational databases.
Option1: Use Amazon Neptune for managed graph database with efficient relationship queries
Option2: Use Amazon RDS with complex JOIN queries
Option3: Use Amazon DynamoDB with adjacency lists
Option4: Use Amazon Redshift for graph queries

QuestionText: A company's application uses multiple AWS services and they want to implement a unified observability solution. Which THREE AWS services provide comprehensive observability? (Select THREE)
Explanation: CloudWatch Metrics provides infrastructure and application metrics with dashboards and alarms. CloudWatch Logs provides centralized log aggregation and analysis with Logs Insights. AWS X-Ray provides distributed tracing showing request flow across services with latency analysis. Together these three pillars of observability (metrics, logs, traces) provide comprehensive visibility into application health and performance.
Option1: Amazon CloudWatch Metrics for infrastructure and application metrics
Option2: Amazon CloudWatch Logs for centralized log aggregation and analysis
Option3: AWS X-Ray for distributed tracing across services
Option4: AWS Config for observability

QuestionText: A company's application uses a self-managed RabbitMQ cluster on EC2 that requires significant operational overhead. They want to reduce management burden while maintaining RabbitMQ compatibility. Which migration should they perform?
Explanation: Amazon MQ for RabbitMQ provides a fully managed RabbitMQ broker. It is wire-protocol compatible—existing applications connect using the same AMQP protocol and client libraries with only an endpoint change. Amazon MQ handles broker provisioning, patching, high availability (Multi-AZ), and monitoring. This eliminates the operational overhead of managing RabbitMQ infrastructure while maintaining full compatibility.
Option1: Migrate to Amazon MQ for RabbitMQ for managed wire-protocol compatible message broker
Option2: Rewrite the application to use SQS
Option3: Continue managing RabbitMQ on EC2
Option4: Switch to Amazon Kinesis

QuestionText: A company is migrating a legacy application that uses a commercial ETL tool (Informatica) for data integration. They want to replace it with AWS-native services. Which TWO services replace Informatica's core capabilities? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Glue replaces Informatica's ETL capabilities—serverless Spark-based data transformation with visual authoring (Glue Studio), data catalog, and job scheduling. Step Functions replaces Informatica's workflow orchestration—coordinating multi-step data pipelines with error handling, retries, and conditional logic. Together they provide the core ETL and orchestration capabilities without commercial licensing costs.
Option1: AWS Glue for serverless ETL data transformation and cataloging
Option2: AWS Step Functions for data pipeline orchestration
Option3: Amazon RDS for ETL processing
Option4: Amazon S3 for data transformation

QuestionText: A company is modernizing their application and wants to implement event-driven architecture. Currently services communicate through direct API calls creating tight coupling. Which TWO AWS services enable event-driven decoupling? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Amazon EventBridge provides a serverless event bus that routes events between services based on content-based rules. Producers publish events without knowing who consumes them. Amazon SNS provides pub/sub messaging where a single event is delivered to multiple subscribers independently. Both decouple producers from consumers—producers don't need to know about consumers, and adding new consumers requires no producer changes.
Option1: Amazon EventBridge for content-based event routing between services
Option2: Amazon SNS for pub/sub fan-out to multiple consumers
Option3: Direct API calls between services
Option4: Shared database for inter-service communication

QuestionText: A company needs to design a solution that provides a managed service for running Apache Kafka with minimal operational overhead. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka) provides fully managed Apache Kafka clusters. MSK handles broker provisioning, patching, and monitoring. It supports native Kafka APIs so existing producers, consumers, and Kafka Connect connectors work without code changes. MSK Serverless eliminates capacity planning entirely—it automatically scales based on throughput. This provides Kafka compatibility with minimal operations.
Option1: Use Amazon MSK for managed Apache Kafka or MSK Serverless for fully serverless Kafka
Option2: Install Kafka on EC2 instances
Option3: Use Amazon Kinesis as a Kafka replacement
Option4: Use Amazon SQS for streaming workloads

QuestionText: A company needs to implement a solution where their AWS environment supports private marketplace to restrict which third-party products teams can purchase from AWS Marketplace. Which service provides this?
Explanation: AWS Marketplace Private Marketplace allows administrators to create a curated catalog of approved third-party products. Teams can only purchase products from the private marketplace—unapproved products are not visible or purchasable. This ensures compliance with procurement policies, security requirements, and licensing agreements while still allowing teams to self-service approved software.
Option1: Configure AWS Marketplace Private Marketplace with a curated catalog of approved products
Option2: Allow unrestricted access to all Marketplace products
Option3: Block all Marketplace access
Option4: Review each purchase request manually

QuestionText: A company's application uses a traditional three-tier architecture with separate web application and database tiers. They want to implement infrastructure as code for repeatable deployments. Which approach provides the MOST comprehensive IaC solution?
Explanation: AWS CDK (Cloud Development Kit) defines infrastructure using familiar programming languages (TypeScript, Python, Java) with high-level constructs that configure multiple resources with sensible defaults. CDK synthesizes CloudFormation templates, providing the reliability of CloudFormation with the expressiveness of programming languages. CDK supports all AWS services and enables code reuse through construct libraries.
Option1: Use AWS CDK for infrastructure as code with programming language constructs that synthesize to CloudFormation
Option2: Manually configure resources through the AWS Console
Option3: Use shell scripts to call AWS CLI commands
Option4: Document the architecture in a wiki for manual recreation
